findings.title: Token expiry, single-use redemption, and generator validation satisfy criterion 1
findings.claim: The committed in-memory token store uses an injected clock and token generator, rejects redemption at or after expiry, prevents reuse, and rejects empty or duplicate generated tokens.
findings.rationale: The supplied final artifact implements expiry and explicit active/redeeming/consumed states. Deterministic tests cover the exact expiry boundary, default lifetime, successful single use, repeated redemption, invalid lifetimes, and invalid generated tokens. The committed suite passed.
findings.remediation: None for the declared single-process demonstration scope.

findings.title: Neutral response and deferred account-dependent work satisfy criterion 2
findings.claim: Known and unknown requests return the same neutral response and enqueue exactly one job before any lookup, issuance, or delivery occurs.
findings.rationale: The committed handler always calls the injected scheduler once and places account lookup, issuance, and delivery inside the scheduled callback. Tests compare known and unknown responses and scheduled counts and verify that lookup and delivery have not run on the response path.
findings.remediation: None for the deterministic injected-scheduler demonstration.

findings.title: Identifier-safe telemetry satisfies criteria 3 and 4
findings.claim: Reset tokens, email addresses, and account identifiers are excluded from application logs and audit payloads, while a constant safe audit event is submitted.
findings.rationale: The handler emits only a constant log message and the constant audit event password_reset_request_accepted with result accepted. Tests compare complete telemetry for known and unknown accounts and assert that synthetic tokens, emails, account IDs, and long hexadecimal values are absent.
findings.remediation: None within scope. A production audit sink would need independent durability and delivery monitoring.

findings.title: Deterministic redemption state transition satisfies criteria 5 and 6
findings.claim: Concurrent redemption attempts yield exactly one success, and consumption occurs only after a successful reset action; rejection reactivates a still-valid token for retry.
findings.rationale: The store changes active to redeeming synchronously before its first await, rejects another attempt while reserved, and changes to consumed only after callback success. On callback rejection it returns to active. Tests deterministically hold the first action open, reject the second attempt, and verify retry after a failed action.
findings.remediation: Callers must retain the documented rollback-or-idempotency contract for reset actions.

findings.title: Dependency-failure isolation satisfies criterion 7
findings.claim: Scheduling, lookup, issuance, delivery, logging, and audit failures preserve the neutral response without leaking failure details into telemetry; a stalled audit transport also does not delay the response.
findings.rationale: Each dependency boundary is isolated. Account-dependent failures are contained in scheduled work, scheduler and logging exceptions are caught, and audit submission is best-effort with synchronous and asynchronous failures contained. Tests exercise every listed dependency failure and the stalled-audit regression.
findings.remediation: None for response-path behavior. Production systems should monitor failed asynchronous work through a non-identifying channel.

findings.title: Deterministic automated coverage satisfies criterion 8
findings.claim: The committed suite contains the required deterministic cases and passes 16 tests with no failures.
findings.rationale: The supplied test artifact covers known and unknown accounts, equal scheduling, deferred lookup and delivery, response equality, safe telemetry, invalid lifetimes, empty and duplicate tokens, unknown tokens, exact expiry, repeated and concurrent redemption, retry after action failure, all enumerated dependency failures, and stalled audit delivery. The post-commit npm test passed at commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4.

findings.title: Final evidence handoff is integrity-bound and complete
findings.claim: The final source, tests, task, and package manifest are bound to a clean scoped commit with artifact hashes and a passing post-commit test.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record identifies commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4, reports scopedClean true, embeds the covered artifacts and SHA-256 digests, and references the passing post-commit test. This supersedes the earlier pre-commit status showing two modified files and removes any apparent status contradiction.

limitations: Atomic redemption is guaranteed only within one JavaScript process. Multi-process or restart-safe production use requires a persistent shared store with transactional compare-and-set semantics, as explicitly documented in the committed artifacts.
The scheduler is an injected demonstration boundary rather than a durable production queue; bounded retries, monitoring, and durable failure handling are outside the declared scope.
Audit submission is best-effort and deliberately not awaited, so the implementation proves a safe audit attempt without guaranteeing durable recording when the audit transport stalls or fails.
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, hosted verification, deployment proof, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not claimed by this review.

findings.title: Password-reset controls satisfy the supplied demonstration criteria
findings.claim: The committed implementation provides expiring, single-use tokens; deterministic single-process concurrent redemption; neutral account-independent responses; deferred account-dependent work; identifier-free telemetry; and failure isolation within the declared synthetic scope.
findings.rationale: The final artifact reserves tokens synchronously before awaiting the reset action, rejects tokens at or after expiry, consumes them only after success, and reactivates valid tokens after callback rejection. Each request enqueues one worker before returning the same frozen public response. Logs and audit details are constant and exclude email addresses, account identifiers, tokens, and dependency error details. The committed 16-test suite covers these behaviors and passed post-commit.
findings.remediation: No remediation is required for the supplied demonstration scope.

findings.title: Final evidence is bound to a clean committed state
findings.claim: The reviewed source, tests, task definition, and package manifest are hash-bound to commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4, with a passing post-commit test and clean scoped state.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record identifies the commit, artifact paths and SHA-256 digests, reports scopedClean=true, and references the successful post-commit test. The source and test hashes match the earlier validation output.
findings.remediation: No remediation is required for the supplied evidence-integrity claim.

limitations: The token store is intentionally in-memory and coordinates redemption only within one JavaScript process. Multi-process or restart-safe production use requires a persistent shared store with an atomic transaction or compare-and-set operation; this is explicitly outside the demonstration scope.

Token entropy depends on the injected generateToken implementation. The supplied synthetic generator is deterministic for testing and is not evidence of a production cryptographically secure generator.

The scheduler is an injected contract. Production durability, bounded retries, monitoring, rate limiting, abuse controls, delivery-channel security, and deployment configuration were not demonstrated.
Audit submission is best-effort and intentionally does not delay the neutral response. The evidence proves safe invocation and failure isolation, not durable audit persistence.
Repository-wide validation, hosted behavior, deployment controls, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not covered by this verdict.
No dependency vulnerability scan or production authentication and authorization integration was supplied; the reviewed package manifest contains no declared dependencies.

findings.title: Acceptance criteria have direct deterministic test coverage
findings.claim: The committed suite covers the required positive, negative, boundary, concurrency, failure-isolation, and regression scenarios for the synthetic password-reset demonstration.
findings.rationale: The 16 tests exercise known and unknown accounts, equal scheduling, off-response-path lookup and delivery, neutral response equality, identifier-free telemetry, exact expiry, repeated and concurrent redemption, retry after callback failure, invalid lifetimes, invalid generated tokens, unknown tokens, and all listed dependency failures. The added stalled-audit regression specifically verifies that audit transport cannot delay the response.
findings.remediation: None required for the declared synthetic scope.

findings.title: Tests pass against the final committed artifacts
findings.claim: The complete workspace test command passed after commit, and the tested artifacts are bound to a clean scoped Git state.
findings.rationale: The capture records a post-commit npm test result at commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4 with exit code 0. The final Git-state record marks the declared paths clean and binds source, tests, task, and package manifest by SHA-256 digest. The preceding detailed run reports 16 passing tests and no failures.

findings.title: Production queue and multi-process atomicity remain outside demonstrated coverage
findings.claim: The tests establish behavior only with a deterministic in-process scheduler and token store; they do not establish durable queue behavior, restart recovery, or atomic redemption across processes.
findings.rationale: The task explicitly defines these as production boundaries rather than acceptance requirements for this synthetic workspace. Both implementation documentation and the task state that production requires a durable queue and persistent shared transactional or compare-and-set storage.
findings.remediation: If this demonstration is promoted toward production, add integration tests against the selected durable queue and shared token store, including retries, process restarts, duplicate delivery, and cross-worker redemption races.

limitations: No hosted, deployment, release, repository-wide canonical-validation, or human-acceptance evidence was supplied; these surfaces were explicitly outside the isolated workspace scope.

The post-commit test evidence reports command success but not per-test output; the detailed 16-test output comes from the immediately preceding scoped validation, while artifact hashes and the final Git-state record provide the integrity linkage.
No real email provider, durable scheduler, persistent database, multi-process worker, or restart-recovery integration tests are included; this does not contradict the declared demonstration criteria.

findings.title: Acceptance criteria are covered by the committed implementation and tests
findings.claim: The scoped password-reset demonstration implements token expiry and single use, neutral responses, deferred account-dependent work, identifier-safe telemetry, deterministic single-process concurrent redemption, retry after failed reset action, and dependency-failure isolation.
findings.rationale: The final-state source and complete test artifact directly implement the stated criteria. The suite includes 16 focused tests covering the required success, boundary, concurrency, telemetry, scheduling, and failure cases. The committed state is bound to a post-commit passing test.

findings.title: Final-state provenance and scoped cleanliness are established
findings.claim: The reviewed artifacts are tied to commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4, have recorded SHA-256 digests, are scoped clean, and passed the post-commit test command.
findings.rationale: The final-git-state record names the final commit, scope paths, artifact contents and hashes, reports scopedClean=true, and references the passing post-commit test. The source and test hashes also match the earlier validation handoff.

findings.title: Audit delivery is best-effort rather than durable
findings.claim: The handler invokes a safe, identifier-free audit event without delaying or changing the neutral response, but this demonstration does not guarantee that an unavailable audit sink durably records it.
findings.rationale: The final implementation starts the audit promise and suppresses synchronous and asynchronous transport failures. This satisfies the demonstrated response-isolation behavior and tests prove invocation and safe contents, including a stalled transport, but durable recording cannot be inferred when the audit dependency fails. The task itself excludes production infrastructure, so this is advisory rather than a readiness failure for the supplied demonstration scope.
findings.remediation: For production, send the safe event through a durable, monitored outbox or queue while preserving the neutral response path.

limitations: Atomic reservation and token persistence are limited to one JavaScript process and do not survive restart; multi-worker production use requires a shared persistent store with transactional or compare-and-set semantics. This limitation is explicitly documented in the final source and task artifact (ev_final_git_state_c06fcf33d345).

The scheduler behavior is demonstrated with an injected deterministic deferred scheduler. Durability, retries, monitoring, and behavior across production workers are outside the declared scope (ev_final_git_state_c06fcf33d345).
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, hosted verification, deployment proof, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not established by this evidence (ev_000014).

The post-commit test record reports command, commit, exit code, and pass status but not individual test output; individual 16/16 results are supplied by the matching pre-commit validation, while the final-state record binds the artifacts and post-commit result (ev_post_commit_test_c06fcf33d345, ev_000013, ev_final_git_state_c06fcf33d345).

output.summary: All four specialist reviews pass the supplied task. Within the declared single-process synthetic demonstration scope, the evidence supports all eight acceptance criteria: expiring single-use tokens, neutral account-independent responses, deferred account work, identifier-safe telemetry, deterministic redemption behavior, failure isolation, and automated coverage. The final artifacts are hash-bound to clean scoped commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4 with a passing post-commit test. Readiness does not extend to production durability, multi-process atomicity, deployment, or durable audit recording.

findings.title: Demonstration acceptance criteria are satisfied
findings.claim: The committed password-reset demonstration satisfies acceptance criteria AC1 through AC8 within its declared deterministic, single-process scope.
findings.rationale: The specialist reviews consistently report coverage of expiry and single use, neutral responses, deferred account-dependent processing, identifier-safe telemetry, deterministic concurrent redemption, retry following reset-action failure, dependency-failure isolation, and the required automated scenarios. The detailed scoped validation reports 16 passing tests, and the final commit has a passing post-commit test result.
findings.remediation: None for the supplied demonstration scope.

findings.title: Final evidence handoff has adequate scoped integrity
findings.claim: The reviewed source, tests, task definition, and package manifest are bound by recorded hashes to a clean scoped commit and a successful post-commit test.
findings.rationale: The final-state record identifies commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4, records artifact SHA-256 digests, reports scopedClean=true, and links the final state to the successful post-commit test. It supersedes the earlier pre-commit modified-file status, so there is no unresolved state contradiction in the supplied handoff.
findings.remediation: None for the narrow evidence-integrity claim.

findings.title: Multi-process durability and atomicity are not demonstrated
findings.claim: The demonstrated scheduler and token store do not establish durable job processing, restart recovery, persistent token state, or atomic redemption across multiple processes.
findings.rationale: The supplied artifacts and specialist reviews explicitly limit the implementation to an injected scheduler and in-memory, single-JavaScript-process coordination. This is consistent with the declared scope and therefore does not block readiness, but it prevents extrapolation to production workers or restart-safe operation.
findings.remediation: Before production use, adopt a durable monitored queue and persistent shared token store with transactional compare-and-set semantics; test retries, duplicate delivery, restarts, and cross-worker redemption races.

findings.title: Audit delivery is safe but best-effort
findings.claim: The demonstration supports identifier-free audit invocation without delaying or changing the neutral response, but does not guarantee durable recording when the audit transport stalls or fails.
findings.rationale: The audit operation is intentionally not awaited, and synchronous and asynchronous transport failures are suppressed to preserve response isolation. Tests cover safe payloads and stalled transport behavior, but these properties cannot establish persistence at an unavailable sink. Durable audit infrastructure is outside the declared scope.
findings.remediation: For production, use a durable monitored outbox or queue for the safe audit event while keeping it off the public response path.

limitations: This synthesis is an assessment of supplied evidence and specialist model judgments, not certification or independent execution.
No tools were available to execute tests or inspect artifacts beyond the supplied digest.

The post-commit evidence records command success rather than individual test output; the detailed 16-test result comes from the preceding scoped validation, with final artifact hashes and commit state providing the stated linkage.
Token entropy in production depends on the injected generator; the deterministic synthetic generator is not evidence of a cryptographically secure production generator.

Rate limiting, abuse prevention, real delivery-provider security, production authentication and authorization integration, dependency vulnerability scanning, and deployment configuration were not demonstrated.
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, hosted verification, deployment proof, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not covered by the readiness verdict.
